questions.question: Explain the four key criteria that requirements must satisfy for effective validation. Provide one example of a requirement that fails each criterion.
keyPoints: Completeness: All stakeholder needs and system requirements captured (example: missing security requirements)
Consistency: No contradictions between requirements (example: two requirements stating opposite behaviors)
Feasibility: Achievable within technical and economic constraints (example: requiring response time of 0.001 seconds for complex queries)
Testability: Can be verified through testing (example: 'system shall be user-friendly' cannot be objectively tested)

questions.question: Describe the IEEE 1028 software inspection process, including its six phases and the roles of team members.
keyPoints: Planning: Define scope, assemble team, schedule meeting
Overview: Present background to reviewers
Preparation: Individual review to find defects
Inspection Meeting: Discuss findings, list defects, make decisions
Rework: Author fixes identified defects
Follow-up: Verify corrections, ensure exit criteria met
Roles: Author (creates work), Reader (presents), Moderator (leads), Reviewers (examine), Scribe (records)

questions.question: Calculate the cost savings if 25 requirements defects are discovered during validation (cost: $300/defect) rather than during system integration testing (cost multiplier: 18x). Show your calculation.
keyPoints: Cost at validation: 25 × $300 = $7,500
The multiplier effect demonstrates importance of early validation
Alternative interpretation: If multiplier applies to base cost: $300 × 18 = $5,400 per defect, total = $135,000

questions.question: What is a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)? Explain its types and how it contributes to requirements validation.
keyPoints: RTM is a table mapping requirements to project artifacts
Forward traceability: Requirements → Design → Code → Tests
Backward traceability: Tests → Code → Design → Requirements
Benefits: Ensures complete coverage, identifies missing links, supports change impact analysis
Validation purpose: Confirms every requirement can be verified and no requirement is orphaned

questions.question: Compare requirements reviews, prototyping, and test case generation as validation techniques. Include advantages and limitations of each.
keyPoints: Reviews: Manual examination by stakeholders; good for completeness but time-consuming
Prototyping: Early visualization of requirements; excellent for UI validation but limited for backend
Test case generation: Forces quantification; directly validates testability but requires expertise
Reviews + Prototyping + Test Generation provide comprehensive validation coverage

questions.question: Identify at least five indicators of ambiguous requirements and explain how each can be resolved during validation.
keyPoints: Vague terms (adequate, sufficient): Define specific measurable values
Undefined pronouns (it, this): Use explicit noun references
Missing units (response time): Add measurement units (seconds, milliseconds)
Unbounded quantities (any, several): Specify exact numbers
Missing conditions (if applicable): State clear triggering conditions
Undefined acronyms: Add glossary or expand all acronyms

questions.question: A software company is developing an e-commerce system. During requirements validation, they encounter the following requirement: 'The system shall have good performance even during peak shopping seasons.' Evaluate this requirement against validation criteria and recommend validation techniques to improve it. Provide a revised version of the requirement.
keyPoints: This requirement fails Testability (vague 'good performance'), Feasibility (no defined constraints), Completeness (what is peak load?)
Techniques: Test case generation (must define measurable metrics), Prototyping (load testing), Requirements review (peer analysis)
Revised requirement should include: response time < 3 seconds for 10,000 concurrent users, throughput of 500 transactions/second, degradation < 20% when load increases by 50%
Should also specify what 'peak season' means (e.g., Black Friday traffic: 50,000 users/hour)
The validation process should involve performance testing team early

questions.question: Explain the cost-benefit analysis of requirements validation. Using a numerical example with 100 requirements and an estimated 10% defect rate, demonstrate why early validation is economically justified. Assume fixing a defect costs $200 in validation, $1,500 in coding, and $8,000 in maintenance.
keyPoints: Total defects = 100 × 10% = 10 defects
Cost if fixed in validation = 10 × $200 = $2,000
Cost if fixed in coding = 10 × $1,500 = $15,000
Cost if fixed in maintenance = 10 × $8,000 = $80,000
Savings from validation vs. coding = $13,000 (87% savings)
Savings from validation vs. maintenance = $78,000 (97.5% savings)
ROI = ($80,000 - $2,000) / $2,000 = 39x return on validation investment
Industry studies show 30-50x cost multiplier between validation and maintenance phases

questions.question: Describe formal specification methods for requirements validation. Explain how mathematical/logical techniques like Z notation or model checking can verify consistency and completeness. Discuss when these techniques are practical versus when informal methods suffice.
keyPoints: Formal specification uses mathematical notation for precise requirements
Z notation: Set theory and predicate logic for state-based specifications
Model checking: Automated verification of state spaces against properties
Consistency verification: Mathematical proof that no contradictions exist
Completeness verification: Check all possible states are defined
Practical for: Safety-critical systems (air traffic control, medical devices), complex protocol specifications
Informal sufficient for: Small projects, prototypes, non-critical business systems
Limitations: Requires specialized expertise, higher initial cost, steep learning curve
